results.possibleIdentity: Paul F. Damaz (1917–2008) was a prominent New York-based architect, author, and critic known for his advocacy for the integration of art and architecture. Born in Portugal on November 8, 1917, he served as a captain in the French Army from 1941 to 1945 before moving to the United States in 1947, where he was naturalized in 1953. Damaz's architectural career in New York included roles as a designer for the United Nations Headquarters (1948–1951) and with the firm Harrison & Abramovitz (1951–1953). He served as chief designer for Cajetan Baumann (1953–1961) before forming the partnership Damaz & Weigel (1962–1976). In 1976, he co-founded Adasco Tech International with civil engineer Ergin Talimci to provide integrated architectural and engineering services. He later operated Paul Damaz Associates in East Hampton. Damaz was a Fellow of the American Institute of Architects (AIA) and a past vice president of the Architectural League of New York. He gained international recognition for his books 'Art in European Architecture' (1956) and 'Art in Latin American Architecture' (1963), which featured prefaces by Le Corbusier and Oscar Niemeyer, respectively. His work and theories on the 'synthesis of the arts'—the collaborative effort between artists, architects, and engineers—aligned with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), with which he was associated during the 1960s and 1970s.
